--- Page 1 ---
Volume Formulae for the Convex Hull of the Graph of a Trilinear
Monomial: A Complete Characterization for General Box Domains
Lillian Makhoul and Emily Speakman
Department of Mathematical and Statistical Sciences
University of Colorado Denver, Denver, CO USA
lillian.makhoul@ucdenver.edu
emily.speakman@ucdenver.edu
Abstract
Solving difficult mixed-integer nonlinear programs via spatial branch-and-bound requires ef-
fective convex outer-approximations of nonconvex sets. In this framework, complex problem
formulations are decomposed into simpler library functions, whose relaxations are then com-
posed to build relaxations of the overall problem. The trilinear monomial serves as one such
fundamental library function, appearing frequently as a building block across diverse applica-
tions. By definition, its convex hull provides the tightest possible relaxation and thus serves as a
benchmark for evaluating alternatives. Mixed volume techniques have yielded a parameterized
volume formula for the convex hull of the graph of a trilinear monomial; however, existing results
only address the case where all six bounds of the box domain are nonnegative. This restriction
represents a notable gap in the literature, as variables with mixed-sign domains arise naturally
in practice. In this work, we close the gap by extending to the general case via an exhaustive
case analysis. We demonstrate that removing the nonnegative domain assumption alters the
underlying structure of the convex hull polytope, leading to six distinct volume formulae that
together characterize all possible parameter configurations.
1
Introduction
Mixed-integer nonlinear programs (MINLPs) are a broad class of optimization problems arising
across numerous domains of science, engineering, and economics. The general framework com-
bines the modeling power of nonlinear and potentially nonconvex functions with discrete decision
variables, enabling formulations that can effectively capture many real-world complexities.
Finding provably globally optimal solutions to MINLPs is a fundamental challenge in com-
putational optimization. Despite the inherent complexity, solvers such as BARON [Sahinidis, 1996],
COUENNE [Belotti et al., 2009], ANTIGONE [Misener and Floudas, 2014], SCIP [Bestuzheva et al.,
2025], and others, are able to optimize small to moderately sized instances. The primary algorith-
mic framework underlying each of these solvers is spatial branch-and-bound (sBB), which at its
core operates via three key steps:
1. Function Decomposition: A fundamental assumption of sBB is that complex functions in
the problem formulation are ‚Äúfactorable‚Äù [McCormick, 1976]. That is, each function can be
systematically decomposed against a given library of simple, often low-dimensional, functions
into a directed acyclic graph (DAG).
2. Convex Relaxations: A second key assumption of sBB is that for every function in the given
1
arXiv:2512.13964v1  [math.OC]  16 Dec 2025


--- Page 2 ---
library, we have a method by which we can construct a valid convex outer-approximation
(i.e., relaxation) of the function‚Äôs graph over a particular domain. Given an appropriate func-
tion decomposition, the convex relaxations of library functions are composed by introducing
auxiliary variables to represent intermediate function values according to the DAG structure;
see Figure 1 for an example. By composing relaxations, the algorithm constructs a valid
relaxation of the original problem formulation which is used to bound the optimal objective
value.
3. Recursive Refinement: Once initial bounds are obtained, sBB iteratively refines the solution
space through ‚Äúspatial branching‚Äù, dividing selected variable domains into smaller subregions.
For each subregion, tighter convex relaxations are constructed, leading to sharper bounds on
the objective function. By recursively applying this process, the algorithm narrows the gap
between upper and lower bounds until some convergence criterion is met.
For a more detailed discussion of sBB, see Ryoo and Sahinidis [1995, 1996], Adjiman et al. [1998],
Smith and Pantelides [1999], Tawarmalani and Sahinidis [2002].
f = ln(5x1)2tan(x2)
x2x2/3
3
(¬∑) √ó (¬∑) √ó (¬∑)
y1 = ln(5x1)
ln(¬∑)
y2 = 2tan(x2)
2(¬∑)
y3 =
1
x2x2/3
3
1
(¬∑)
y4 = 5x1
5 √ó (¬∑)
y5 = tan(x2)
tan(¬∑)
y6 = x2x2/3
3
(¬∑) √ó (¬∑)
y7 = x2/3
3
(¬∑)2/3
x3 ‚àà[a3, b3]
x1 ‚àà[a1, b1]
x2 ‚àà[a2, b2]
Figure 1: Example of a nonconvex function decomposed (factored) against the library functions
shown.
The efficiency of sBB fundamentally depends on the convexifications used to approximate the
graphs of library functions. Problem relaxations are solved at every node of the branch-and-bound
tree, and tighter convexifications yield stronger bounds on the objective value, allowing the algo-
rithm to prune more of the search space and reduce overall tree size. However, a natural tradeoff
exists between relaxation tightness and computational overhead. The convex hull may require com-
plex inequality descriptions that are expensive to optimize over. At the same time, because con-
vexifications are composed throughout the expression DAG, improvements at the library-function
level benefit the entire model, while weak relaxations compound throughout the formulation.
2


--- Page 3 ---
1.1
Using volume to compare alternative convex relaxations
Given that the choice of convexification method significantly impacts sBB performance, the analytic
comparison of alternative methods is desirable. In the literature, the (n + 1)-dimensional volume
of the convex hull of a library function‚Äôs graph has served as a baseline for such comparisons. This
is intuitive: the convex hull, by definition, has the smallest volume among all convex relaxations.
Thus, the closer in volume a relaxation is to the hull, the tighter its approximation. Experimental
results in Speakman et al. [2017] and Lee et al. [2022] provide strong evidence that choosing library
function convexifications with a smaller volume leads to improved objective function bounds when
solving the overall problem relaxation.
Volume-based comparison of mathematical optimization formulations was introduced by Lee
and Morris [1994], who studied various combinatorial polytopes. See also Steingr¬¥ƒ±msson [1994] for
volume results on vertex-packing polytopes and Ko et al. [1997] for work on relaxations of the
boolean-quadric polytope. However, the focus of these early works was not on the low dimensional
functions of interest in sBB. Volume-based comparison of library function convexifications in the
sBB context was considered by Cafieri et al. [2010], who computationally investigated alternative
relaxations of quadrilinear terms. Beyond computational studies, volume has also been employed for
the analytical comparison of low-dimensional sets. For instance, Lee et al. [2022, 2023] compared
convex relaxations of simple disjunctions, including those obtained via the perspective function
reformulation.
The general multilinear monomial g(x1, . . . , xn) := x1x2 . . . xn on a box domain [a1, b1] √ó
[a2, b2]√ó¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑√ó[an, bn] is a key substructure in many nonconvex optimization problems; consequently,
convex relaxations of its graph have been extensively studied. Relevant contributions include convex
envelope characterizations [Rikun, 1997, Meyer and Floudas, 2005, Jach et al., 2008], and analysis
of the strength of various alternative polyhedral relaxations [Ryoo and Sahinidis, 2001, Luedtke
et al., 2012, Del Pia and Khajavirad, 2021, Khajavirad, 2023, Schutte and Walter, 2024].
Prior research has also explicitly considered facet descriptions and volume formulae for re-
laxations of low-dimensional multilinear terms over a box domain. There has been a particular
focus on the trilinear case of n = 3, which is highly relevant to this work. See Meyer and Floudas
[2004a,b] for facet characterizations, as well as Speakman and Lee [2017], Speakman et al. [2017],
Lee et al. [2018], Speakman and Averkov [2019] for volume analyses. The present work extends
these contributions by generalizing a result from Speakman and Lee [2017] using the techniques of
Speakman and Averkov [2019].
We consider the trilinear monomial f(x1, x2, x3) := x1x2x3 with xi ‚àà[ai, bi], ai < bi for
i = 1, 2, 3, and without loss of generality, label the variables so that the conditions
a1b2 ‚â§b1a2,
a1b3 ‚â§b1a3,
and
a2b3 ‚â§b2a3
(‚Ñ¶)
are satisfied.
We also define the convex hull of the graph of f (parameterized by the 3 √ó 2 = 6 variable
bounds) as
P3
H(a, b) := conv{(f, x1, x2, x3) ‚ààR4 : f = x1x2x3, xi ‚àà[ai, bi], i = 1, 2, 3}.
It is well-known that P3
H(a, b) is polyhedral and has 23 = 8 extreme points, see Rikun [1997],
for example. Using this structure, Speakman and Lee [2017] construct a triangulation of P3
H(a, b)
and derive a formula for its volume in terms of the six parameters defining the box domain. The
result assumes all variable bounds are nonnegative.
3


--- Page 4 ---
Theorem 1 (Theorem 4.1 in Speakman and Lee [2017]). Assume 0 ‚â§ai < bi for i = 1, 2, 3 and
(‚Ñ¶) holds. Then
Vol
␀P3
H(a, b)

= (b1 ‚àía1)(b2 ‚àía2)(b3 ‚àía3)√ó
(b1(5b2b3 ‚àía2b3 ‚àíb2a3 ‚àí3a2a3) + a1(5a2a3 ‚àíb2a3 ‚àía2b3 ‚àí3b2b3))/24.
Furthermore, Theorem 1 was subsequently re-proved via an alternative method. Speakman
and Averkov [2019] derive the result utilizing tools from mixed volume theory.
1.2
Our contribution
Despite receiving significant attention, a notable gap remains in the literature with regard to existing
volume formulae for P3
H(a, b). Both Speakman and Lee [2017] and Speakman and Averkov [2019]
restrict attention to nonnegative variable bounds, i.e., ai ‚â•0 for i = 1, 2, 3. This leaves an open
question: can closed-form volume formulae be derived for general mixed-sign domains? In this work,
we address that gap by extending the existing results to general box domains via an exhaustive
case analysis. Employing the mixed volume framework of Speakman and Averkov [2019], we derive
explicit volume formulae for all parameter configurations.
1.3
Organization
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Sections 2‚Äì5 provide the required preliminaries,
beginning by defining our notation in Section 2. Section 3 reviews the necessary background from
mixed volume theory, and Section 4 provides some useful geometric insight. Section 5 establishes
the foundational results required for our main theorem, which is presented in Section 6. We end
with brief concluding remarks and suggestions for future work in Section 7. The appendix contains
the technical lemmas required for our main results, along with complete proofs.
2
Notation
We use standard notation for polytopes, including conv(¬∑) to denote the convex hull, and write
E(P) for the set of extreme points of a polytope P. For a convex body B ‚äÇRd, vold (B) denotes
its d-dimensional volume (i.e., Lebesgue measure). We write Kn to denote the set of all nonempty
compact convex sets in Rn. Vectors are denoted in boldface (e.g., v, u).
Throughout this paper, we work with box domains: xi ‚àà[ai, bi] where ai < bi for i = 1, 2, 3. In
the literature, this notation (that is, characterizing the variable intervals by their lower and upper
bounds) has been used extensively in prior work on the trilinear case, including facet descriptions of
P3
H [Meyer and Floudas, 2004b,a], inequality descriptions of various natural relaxations [Speakman
and Lee, 2017], and related volume formulae [Speakman and Lee, 2017, Lee et al., 2018, Speakman
and Averkov, 2019]. However, intervals can equivalently be characterized by their center point and
half-length.
For i = 1, 2, 3, define
ci := ai + bi
2
and
‚Ñìi := bi ‚àíai
2
,
so that ai = ci ‚àí‚Ñìi, bi = ci + ‚Ñìi, and xi ‚àà[ci ‚àí‚Ñìi, ci + ‚Ñìi].
We then write
P3
H(c, ‚Ñì) := conv

(f, x1, x2, x3) ‚ààR4 : f = x1x2x3, xi ‚àà[ci ‚àí‚Ñìi, ci + ‚Ñìi], i = 1, 2, 3
	
.
4


--- Page 5 ---
Substituting into (‚Ñ¶) and simplifying yields an equivalent formulation of the variable labeling
condition in terms of centers and half-lengths. Since ‚Ñìi > 0 for all i, we obtain
c1
‚Ñì1
‚â§c2
‚Ñì2
‚â§c3
‚Ñì3
.
(‚Ñ¶‚Ä≤)
Using this notation, the result of Speakman and Lee [2017] can be restated as follows.
Theorem 2 (Theorem 4.1 in Speakman and Lee [2017], restated). Suppose 0 < ‚Ñìi ‚â§ci
for
i = 1, 2, 3 and (‚Ñ¶‚Ä≤) holds. Then
vol4

.
This center and half-length notation simplifies exposition and highlights the essential structure
in our analysis. We use it throughout the remainder of the paper.
3
Background
This section reviews the key concepts from mixed volume theory needed for our analysis. The
presentation closely follows Section 2 of Speakman and Averkov [2019], with proofs omitted. For
complete details and proofs, see Speakman and Averkov [2019] and Schneider [2013].
Theorem 3 (Theorem 2.1 in Speakman and Averkov [2019]). There is a unique nonnegative
function V : Kn √ó ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ √ó Kn
|
{z
}
n
‚ÜíR, the mixed volume, which is invariant under permutation of its
arguments, such that, for every positive integer m > 0,
voln (t1K1 + t2K2 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + tmKm) =
m

Theorem 4 (Theorem 2.2 in Speakman and Averkov [2019]). The mixed volume function satisfies
the following properties:
(i) voln (K1) = V (K1, . . . , K1),
(ii) V (t‚Ä≤K‚Ä≤

1 , K2, . . . , Kn),
for K1, K2, . . . , Kn ‚ààKn and t‚Ä≤, t‚Ä≤‚Ä≤ ‚ààR+.
Definition 1 (Support function). For a nonempty compact set K ‚äÜRn, the support function
hK : Rn ‚ÜíR is defined by
hK(u) = sup
x‚ààK
xT u.
Definition 2 (Set of outer facet normals). For a full-dimensional polytope, P ‚äÜRn,
U(P) := the set of all outer facet normals, u, such that the length of u equals
the (n ‚àí1)-dimensional volume of the corresponding facet.

When n = 3 and P ‚äÜR3 is a tetrahedron, we may explicitly derive its set of outer facet normals
using the following result.

4
Geometric insight
The key geometric observation underlying the mixed volume approach becomes clear when viewing
P3
H(c, ‚Ñì) as the convex hull of its extreme points.

Figure 2: Adapted from Speakman and Averkov [2019]. Schlegel diagram of P3
H(c, ‚Ñì), showing its
embedding in the 4-dimensional hypercube. Note that P3
H(c, ‚Ñì) = conv (Q ‚à™R).
Observe that for any variable xi with i ‚àà{1, 2, 3}, we can partition the vertices of P3
H(c, ‚Ñì) into
two sets: four vertices lying in the hyperplane xi = ci ‚àí‚Ñìi, and four vertices lying in the hyperplane
xi = ci + ‚Ñìi. Without loss of generality, we choose i = 3 and denote the convex hulls of these
resulting vertex sets by Q and R, respectively. See Figure 2 for an illustration. Formally,
Q := conv{v1, v2, v3, v4}
and
R := conv{v5, v6, v7, v8}.
By viewing P3
H(c, ‚Ñì) as the convex hull of the union of Q and R, that is, two (n‚àí1 = 3)-dimensional
polytopes lying in parallel hyperplanes, its volume can be expressed as an integral over x3:
vol4
␀P3
H(c, ‚Ñì)



dx3.
Note that at x3 = c3‚àí‚Ñì3, the argument inside the volume function reduces to Q, while at x3 = c3+‚Ñì3,
it reduces to R. At intermediate values, it is a Minkowski sum of scaled copies of Q and R.
Applying Theorems 3 and 4 with n = 3, we obtain

Z c3+‚Ñì3

(c3 + ‚Ñì3 ‚àíx3)3vol3 (Q) + 3(c3 + ‚Ñì3 ‚àíx3)2(x3 ‚àíc3 + ‚Ñì3)V (Q, Q, R)
+ 3(c3 + ‚Ñì3 ‚àíx3)(x3 ‚àíc3 + ‚Ñì3)2V (Q, R, R) + (x3 ‚àíc3 + ‚Ñì3)3vol3 (R) dx3.
(1)
7


--- Page 8 ---
Therefore, to obtain the volume of P3
H(c, ‚Ñì), we compute the volumes of Q and R, and the
mixed volumes V (Q, Q, R) and V (Q, R, R), then evaluate the integral in Equation (1). This is
exactly the approach taken by Speakman and Averkov [2019] to obtain the volume formula for the
case of nonnegative bounds; however, they express (1) in terms of the upper and lower variable
bounds.
Under their assumptions, no case analysis was required when computing the volumes
and mixed volumes, ultimately yielding a single closed-form volume formula for the convex hull
polytope. In contrast, removing the nonnegativity assumption necessitates extensive case analysis,
even under the ordering (‚Ñ¶‚Ä≤).
5
Preliminary results
To reduce the case analysis required, we begin with the following symmetry observation.
Lemma 2. For i = 1, 2, 3, let Àúci = |ci|. Then
vol4
␀P3
H(c, ‚Ñì)

= vol4
␀P3
H(Àúc, ‚Ñì)

.
Proof. For each i ‚àà{1, 2, 3}, let œÉi = sign(ci) ‚àà{‚àí1, +1}, and consider the linear map œï : R4 ‚ÜíR4
defined by
œï(f, x1, x2, x3) := (œÉ1œÉ2œÉ3 ¬∑ f, œÉ1x1, œÉ2x2, œÉ3x3),
with matrix representation
Dœï =
Ô£Æ
Ô£ØÔ£ØÔ£∞
œÉ1œÉ2œÉ3
0
0
0
0
œÉ1
0
0
0

œÉ2
0
0

0
œÉ3
Ô£π
Ô£∫Ô£∫Ô£ª.
Note that because each œÉi ‚àà{‚àí1, +1}, we have det(Dœï) = œÉ2
1œÉ2
2œÉ2
3 = 1, thus the mapping œï
preserves volume. Moreover, we claim œï is a bijection from P3
H(c, ‚Ñì) to P3
H(Àúc, ‚Ñì).
To see this, let (f, x1, x2, x3) ‚ààP3
H(c, ‚Ñì), so that f = x1x2x3 and xi ‚àà[ci ‚àí‚Ñìi, ci + ‚Ñìi] for
i = 1, 2, 3. Setting ( Àúf, Àúx1, Àúx2, Àúx3) = œï(f, x1, x2, x3), we verify:
(i) The constraint Àúf = Àúx1Àúx2Àúx3 holds:
Àúf = œÉ1œÉ2œÉ3 ¬∑ f = œÉ1œÉ2œÉ3(x1x2x3) = (œÉ1x1)(œÉ2x2)(œÉ3x3) = Àúx1Àúx2Àúx3.
(ii) For i = 1, 2, 3, we have Àúxi = œÉixi ‚àà[|ci| ‚àí‚Ñìi, |ci| + ‚Ñìi]:
‚Ä¢ If ci ‚â•0, then œÉi = 1 and Àúxi = xi ‚àà[ci ‚àí‚Ñìi, ci + ‚Ñìi] = [|ci| ‚àí‚Ñìi, |ci| + ‚Ñìi].
‚Ä¢ If ci < 0, then œÉi = ‚àí1 and since xi ‚àà[ci ‚àí‚Ñìi, ci + ‚Ñìi], we have
Àúxi = ‚àíxi ‚àà[‚àí(ci + ‚Ñìi), ‚àí(ci ‚àí‚Ñìi)] = [‚àíci ‚àí‚Ñìi, ‚àíci + ‚Ñìi] = [|ci| ‚àí‚Ñìi, |ci| + ‚Ñìi].
Therefore, œï maps P3
H(c, ‚Ñì) into P3
H(Àúc, ‚Ñì), and because it is invertible with œï‚àí1 = œï, it is a bijection.
Thus, the mapping œï is a volume-preserving bijection and
vol4
␀P3
H(c, ‚Ñì)

= vol4
␀P3
H(Àúc, ‚Ñì)

as required.
For the remainder of the paper, without loss of generality, we assume ci ‚â•0 for i = 1, 2, 3.
8


--- Page 9 ---
5.1
Computing the Volumes of Q and R
To compute the (3-dimensional) volumes of Q and R, we simply need to observe that they are both
3-dimensional simplices, in other words, tetrahedra. The volume of each may be computed using
the standard determinant formula for simplices.
Lemma 3. The volume of Q is given by
vol3 (Q) =
(
8
3‚Ñì2
1‚Ñì2
2 (c3 ‚àí‚Ñì3) ,
if c3
‚Ñì3 ‚â•1,
8
3‚Ñì2
1‚Ñì2
2 (‚Ñì3 ‚àíc3) ,
if c3
‚Ñì3 < 1.
Lemma 4. The volume of R is given by
vol3 (R) = 8
3‚Ñì2
1‚Ñì2
2(c3 + ‚Ñì3).
Note that due to our nonnegativity assumption on c, we have c3 + ‚Ñì3 ‚â•0, and we do not need
to consider separate cases when computing the volume of R.
5.2
Computing the Mixed Volumes V (Q, Q, R) and V (Q, R, R)
By Theorem 5, we have
V (Q, Q, R) = 1
3
X
u‚ààU(Q)
hR(u)
and
V (Q, R, R) = 1
3
X
u‚ààU(R)
hQ(u),
where U(Q) and U(R) denote the sets of outer facet normals of Q and R (Definition 2), and hQ(¬∑)
and hR(¬∑) denote the support functions of Q and R (Definition 1).

Observe that when c3
‚Ñì3 < 1, we must relabel the extreme points of Q in order to satisfy the assump-
tions of Lemma 1. This results in the two distinct cases for U(Q).
5.2.2
Evaluating the support functions
To compute the necessary mixed volumes, we must evaluate hQ(¬∑) at each point in U(R) and
evaluate hR(¬∑) at each point in U(Q).
Recall by Definition 1,
hQ(u) = max
x‚ààQ xT u
and
hR(u) = max
x‚ààR xT u.
Moreover, because Q and R are tetrahedra (embedded in R4), to compute hR(u) or hQ(u) for any
vector u, we need only compute xT u for each of the four extreme points, x, and take the maximum
of these values. Note that because the signs of the outer facet normals in U(Q) depend on the
indicator I, the inner products for the case c3
‚Ñì3 ‚â•1 and c3
‚Ñì3 < 1 differ by a factor of ‚àí1. This leads
to further case analysis when evaluating the mixed volumes.
5.2.3
Putting it together
Throughout the proofs of Propositions 1 and 2, we refer to Lemmas 5‚Äì12 whose statements and
proofs appear in the appendix.

These six cases form a complete partition of the parameter space under the assumptions.
Proof. The result follows from substituting the volumes of Q and R (Lemmas 3 and 4), and the
mixed volumes V (Q, Q, R) and V (Q, R, R) (Propositions 1 and 2) into Equation (1), evaluating the
integral, and simplifying. The six cases in the theorem correspond to different case combinations
that arise when computing these volumes and mixed volumes. For a summary, see Table 1. Recall
that there is no case analysis for the volume of R, hence we use
vol3 (R) = 8
3‚Ñì2
1‚Ñì2
2(c3 + ‚Ñì3),
in every case of the theorem, and Lemma 4 is omitted from the table.
6.1
Example
Consider the polytope
P := conv{(f, x1, x2, x3) ‚ààR4 : f = x1x2x3, x1 ‚àà[3, 7], x2 ‚àà[‚àí2, 4], x3 ‚àà[‚àí3, ‚àí1]}.
To find the volume of P, we first compute the centers and half-lengths of each interval. Applying
Lemma 2, we take c1 = 5, c2 = 1, c3 = 2, and ‚Ñì1 = 2, ‚Ñì2 = 3, ‚Ñì3 = 1.
To ensure the correct labeling, we calculate:
c1
‚Ñì1
= 5
2,

‚Ñì2
= 1

and

‚Ñì3
= 2

tion 1, Proposition 2, and Lemma 3 are used to evaluate the integral in Equation (1) for the given
case of Theorem 6.
and observe that we must permute the indices to satisfy (‚Ñ¶‚Ä≤):
(1, 2, 3) 7‚Üí(3, 1, 2).
Therefore, we define the polytope
ÀúP := conv{(f, x1, x2, x3) ‚ààR4 : f = x1x2x3, x1 ‚àà[‚àí2, 4], x2 ‚àà[1, 3], x3 ‚àà[3, 7]},
and note that vol4 (P) = vol4

by Lemma 2.
The plots in Figure 3 visualize the 4-dimensional polytope ÀúP by displaying five representative
examples (at x3 ‚àà{3, 4, 5, 6, 7}) from the continuum of 3-dimensional cross-sections of ÀúP parallel
to the x3 = 0 plane. Note that when x3 = 3 we have exactly the tetrahedron Q, and when x3 = 7,
we have R. ‚ÄúSlices‚Äù of ÀúP, such as these examples, correspond to the argument inside the volume
function in Equation (1). Specifically, for a fixed x3 ‚àà[3, 7], the corresponding cross-section may
be described as
c3 + ‚Ñì3 ‚àíx3
(c3 + ‚Ñì3) ‚àí(c3 ‚àí‚Ñì3)Q +
x3 ‚àí(c3 ‚àí‚Ñì3)
(c3 + ‚Ñì3) ‚àí(c3 ‚àí‚Ñì3)R
=
7 ‚àíx3
4
Q + x3 ‚àí3
4
R,
and Theorem 6 provides the formula for
vol4

ÀúP

=
Z x3=7
x3=3
vol3
␀ 7‚àíx3
4
Q + x3‚àí3
4
R

dx3.
Taking into account the appropriate labeling, observe that we fall into Case 2 of Theorem 6.
14


--- Page 15 ---
We can now compute the volume of ÀúP, and thus the volume of P, as follows:
vol4 (P) = vol4

ÀúP

= 8
3‚Ñì2
1‚Ñì2
2‚Ñì2


2c2
‚Ñì2
+ 2c3
‚Ñì3
+ 1

= 8
3(3)2(1)2(2)2

2 ¬∑ 2
1 + 2 ¬∑ 5
2 + 1

= 8
3 √ó 36 √ó 10
= 960.
The reader may verify this result numerically using software.
(a) Cross-section when
x3 = 3
(b) Cross-section when
x3 = 4
(c) Cross-section when
x3 = 5
(d) Cross-section when
x3 = 6
(e) Cross-section when
x3 = 7
Figure 3: Cross-sections of ÀúP for x3 ‚àà{3, 4, 5, 6, 7}. Note that when x3 = 3, the corresponding
cross-section is Q, and when x3 = 7, the corresponding cross-section is R.
7
Conclusions and further work
In this paper, we derived closed-form volume formulae for the convex hull of the graph of a trilinear
monomial over general box domains. Under the nonnegativity assumption on our interval centers
15


--- Page 16 ---
(Lemma 2), and the ordering (‚Ñ¶‚Ä≤), the volume divides into six cases determined by the center-to-
half-length ratios ci/‚Ñìi. These cases reflect the different geometric configurations that arise with
mixed-sign domains.
Our work extends existing volume results to general box domains, completing the analysis
initiated by Speakman and Lee [2017] and subsequently reproved by Speakman and Averkov [2019].
While we have completed the volume analysis for the convex hull of the trilinear monomial graph,
volume formulae for natural relaxations of the convex hull over general domains, such as the three
alternative convexifications obtained via an iterative application of the McCormick inequalities
[McCormick, 1976], remain an open research question.
Currently, such formulae exist only for
nonnegative bounds [Speakman and Lee, 2017], where under the ordering condition (‚Ñ¶), the three
convexifications can be ranked according to their volume.
Interesting directions for future work include deriving closed-form volume formulae for these
alternative McCormick-based relaxations over general domains, and determining how they rank by
tightness. It remains open whether the ranking observed for nonnegative bounds [Speakman and
Lee, 2017] persists over general domains, or if mixed-sign configurations alter the relative tightness
of these relaxations. Beyond this, building on the computations in Speakman et al. [2017] and Lee
et al. [2022] to further explore connections to overall global optimization performance represents
an important avenue for future research. Finally, extending these comparisons to higher-degree
monomials remains a compelling, if challenging, open direction.
8
Acknowledments
The authors would like to express their sincere gratitude to Prof. Dr. Gennadiy Averkov for his
insightful contributions to this work, including his valuable suggestion to characterize box domains
via their centers and half-lengths.
E. Speakman further wishes to thank Dr.
Averkov for the
numerous conversations in which he generously shared his expertise, patiently and enthusiastically
explaining important nuances of mixed volume theory.
In addition, the authors gratefully acknowledge summer funding for L. Makhoul from the
University of Colorado Denver Department of Mathematical and Statistical Sciences.

The set of outer facet normals of Q:
U(Q) :=
(
2‚Ñì1‚Ñì2 {u1, u2, u3, u4} ,

‚Ñì3 < 1,
and the set of outer facet normals of R:
U(R) := 2‚Ñì1‚Ñì2 {u5, u6, u7, u8} .
Furthermore, we note that because Q lies in the hyperplane x3 = c3‚àí‚Ñì3 and R lies in the hyperplane
x3 = c3 + ‚Ñì3, their outer facet normals ui are 4-dimensional vectors with a zero component in the
x3 direction (the fourth component). Consequently, when computing vT
j ui for any extreme point
vj, the contribution from the x3 component vanishes, and the inner product depends only on the
first three coordinates.
Finally, recall that without loss of generality, we make the nonnegativity assumption ci ‚â•0 for
i = 1, 2, 3, and by definition ‚Ñìi > 0 for i = 1, 2, 3. Moreover, we assume the ordering (‚Ñ¶‚Ä≤):

Lemma 6. The relevant inner products are given by:
vT
5 u2 = (c1 ‚àí‚Ñì1) (c2 (3‚Ñì3 ‚àíc3) ‚àí‚Ñì2 (c3 + ‚Ñì3)) ,
vT
6 u2 = ‚Ñì1 (‚Ñì2 (‚Ñì3 ‚àí3c3) + c2 (c3 + ‚Ñì3)) ‚àíc1 (‚Ñì2 (c3 + ‚Ñì3) + c2 (c3 ‚àí3‚Ñì3)) ,

8 u2 = (c2 + ‚Ñì2) (‚Ñì1 (c3 + ‚Ñì3) ‚àíc1 (c3 ‚àí3‚Ñì3)) .

z+
2 := max{vT
5 u2, vT
6 u2, vT
7 u2, vT
8 u2} = vT
8 u2,
and
z‚àí
2 := max{‚àívT
5 u2, ‚àívT
6 u2, ‚àívT
7 u2, ‚àívT
8 u2} = ‚àívT
7 u2.
Proof. The inner product formulas follow from direct computation using the definitions of vj and
u2. We show that vT
8 u2 ‚â•vT
j u2 for all j ‚àà{5, 6, 7} by computing the pairwise differences:
vT
8 u2 ‚àívT
5 u2 = 4‚Ñì3(‚Ñì2c1 + ‚Ñì1c2) ‚â•0,
vT
8 u2 ‚àívT
6 u2 = 4‚Ñì2(‚Ñì1c3 + ‚Ñì3c1) ‚â•0,
vT
8 u2 ‚àívT
7 u2 = 4‚Ñì1‚Ñì3(c2 + ‚Ñì2) ‚â•0.
Each inequality holds because of our nonnegativity assumption. Thus vT
8 u2 is the maximum
of the four values.
We now determine the maximum of {‚àívT
5 u2, ‚àívT
6 u2, ‚àívT
7 u2, ‚àívT
8 u2}. Computing pairwise
differences we obtain:
‚àívT
7 u2 ‚àí(‚àívT


‚â•0.
The first inequality holds because (‚Ñ¶‚Ä≤) gives c3

Lemma 7. The relevant inner products are given by:
vT
5 u3 = ‚Ñì1 (‚Ñì2 (‚Ñì3 ‚àí3c3) + c2 (c3 + ‚Ñì3)) + c1 (‚Ñì2 (c3 + ‚Ñì3) + c2 (c3 ‚àí3‚Ñì3)) ,
